1:
"And Saul, yet breathing out threatenings and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord, went unto the high priest," The witnesses note the persecutor's continuing rage as the narrative of his conversion opens. The grammatical voices stress the vivid metaphor. The phrase pictures Saul as one whose very breath was threatening and slaughter — a man wholly given to the destruction of the church, breathing murder as the air of his life.

The Reformed Calvin marks the providence in arresting such a man: "This is surely the most excellent mercy of God, in that that man is reclaimed unto salvation contrary to the purpose of his mind, whom so great a heat carried headlong into destruction." The deeper Saul's rage, the harder his conversion, and so the greater the display of God's grace: "the conversion of Paul was so much the harder, forasmuch as he was already made more obstinate by continuing his fury."

The witnesses note the contrast that frames the whole chapter. Calvin: "it was no easy matter to pull down so great pride, to break such a lofty courage, to pacify such a blind heat of wicked zeal, and, finally, to bridle a most unbridled beast." The man "breathing out slaughter" is the very one Christ would arrest and transform into the chief apostle.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the conversion narrative. The witnesses agree: Saul, still breathing out threatenings and slaughter, went to the high priest for authority to extend the persecution — a man wholly given to the destruction of the church, breathing murder as his very air. Calvin marks the providence: the arrest of such a furious enemy, reclaimed against the whole bent of his mind, is the most excellent mercy of God; the harder his rage, the greater the grace that overcame it. The verse sets the stage for one of the most remarkable conversions in history: the chief persecutor, at the height of his fury, met and transformed by the risen Christ. The contrast between the "breathing out slaughter" of this verse and the bold preaching of Christ that follows (v.20) is the measure of the grace that wrought the change. The verse displays both the depth of human enmity against the gospel and the power of God to subdue and convert even the most violent foe — turning the persecutor into the apostle, the wolf into the shepherd. The man who breathed slaughter would soon suffer for the name he sought to destroy.
2:
"And desired of him letters to Damascus to the synagogues, that if he found any of this way, whether they were men or women, he might bring them bound unto Jerusalem." The witnesses note the extent of Saul's zeal and the first use of "the Way." On the jurisdiction, Barnes: "the sanhedrim at Jerusalem claimed jurisdiction over all synagogues everywhere... the authority of regulating everywhere the Jewish religion." Damascus, the witnesses note, was at this time under Aretas (2 Corinthians 11:32).

On "this way," the grammatical voices note the first occurrence of a key term. Ellicott: "the first occurrence of a term which seems to have been used familiarly as a synonym for the disciples of Christ... It may have originated in the words in which Christ had claimed to be Himself the Way (John 14:6)... Prior to the general acceptance of the term Christian (Acts 11:26) it served as a convenient, neutral designation." Robertson notes the parallel uses (19:9,23; 22:4; 24:14,22).

On the mention of women, the witnesses note the special cruelty. Calvin: "There is mention made of women, that it may the better appear how desirous he was to shed blood who had no respect of sex, whom even armed enemies are wont to spare." Robertson notes this is mentioned three times (8:3; 9:2; 22:4) "as a special blot in Paul's cruelty."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Saul sought letters to the Damascus synagogues to arrest "any of this way" — men or women — and bring them bound to Jerusalem, the Sanhedrin claiming jurisdiction over Jews everywhere (Barnes). The verse marks the first use of "the Way" as a name for the disciples of Christ — a neutral designation, perhaps from Christ's claim to be the Way (John 14:6), prior to the term "Christian" (Ellicott, Robertson). The mention of women persecuted displays the special cruelty of Saul's zeal, sparing not even those whom war spares (Calvin) — a blot he later remembered with shame. The verse displays the lengths of Saul's rage: not content with Jerusalem, he sought to extend the persecution to Damascus, hunting men and women of "the Way" to imprison them. It was on this very errand of destruction that Christ arrested him (v.3). The persecutor, armed with letters and authority, set out to bind the disciples — and was himself bound by the grace of the Lord he persecuted. The Way he sought to destroy became the Way he would proclaim to the ends of the earth.
3:
"And as he journeyed, he came near Damascus: and suddenly there shined round about him a light from heaven:" The witnesses note the sudden heavenly light that arrested Saul — and divide on its nature. The grammatical voices stress the suddenness, like a flash of lightning (Robertson). The witnesses note the three accounts of the conversion (chs. 9, 22, 26) with their variations, which Robertson defends: "In broad outline and in all essentials the three accounts agree and testify to the truthfulness of the account."

On the nature of the light, the witnesses divide. Barnes argues for a supernatural appearance of the glorified Christ — the reappearance of the Shekinah glory: "We are to regard this appearance as the reappearance of the Shecaniah, the Son of God invested with appropriate glory, appearing to convince an enemy of his ascension." He gives strong objections to the thunderstorm theory (the others saw the light but heard not the voice; it was declared an appearance of the Lord, 1 Corinthians 15:8). Clarke, by contrast, suggests "an extraordinary flash of the electric fluid, accompanied with thunder," divinely modified.

Ellicott entertains both, without excluding the supernatural: "Nothing in the narrative suggests the thought of a sudden thunderstorm... If this hypothesis be at all entertained — and as it does not necessarily exclude the supernatural element... we must think of the storm as coming with an almost instantaneous quickness." The Reformed Calvin holds it a manifestation of Christ's divine glory: "Christ must needs have showed some sign of his majesty, whereby Paul might perceive that he had to do with God himself."

The witnesses agree: as Saul neared Damascus, a sudden light from heaven shone round about him, arresting him on his errand of persecution. They divide on its nature: Barnes argues strongly for a supernatural appearance of the glorified Christ in the Shekinah glory (the only view that fits the data — the personal appearance of the Lord, 1 Corinthians 15:8); Clarke suggests a divinely-used flash of lightning and thunder; Ellicott entertains a near-instantaneous thunderstorm that does not exclude the supernatural. Calvin holds it a manifestation of Christ's divine majesty, that Saul might know he had to do with God himself. The verse displays the decisive moment of Saul's conversion: the persecutor struck down by a light above the brightness of the sun (26:13), the risen Christ appearing to arrest his enemy and make him an apostle. The three accounts, with their variations, together attest the reality of the event — the turning point of Saul's life and an epoch in the history of the church.
4:
"And he fell to the earth, and heard a voice saying unto him, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" The witnesses note Saul's prostration and the voice that revealed the mystic union of Christ and his church. On the falling, Barnes notes the parallel with Daniel (Daniel 8:17; 10:8): "The effect was such as to overpower the body." Calvin: "this was the first beginning of the bringing down of Paul, that he might become apt to hear the voice of Christ, which he had despised."

On the doubled name, Barnes notes the emphasis: "The repetition of the name would fix his attention," as in Christ's address to Martha (Luke 10:41) and Simon (Luke 22:31). Ellicott notes the Hebrew form of the name, "as though he, who gloried in being a Hebrew of the Hebrews, heard himself claimed as such by Him who spoke from heaven, called as Samuel had been called of old."

On "why persecutest thou me," the witnesses dwell on the mystic union. Barnes: "Christ and his people are one (John 15:1-6). To persecute them, therefore, was to persecute him." Calvin draws the comfort: "the godly may gather great comfort by this, in that they hear that the Son of God is partner with them of the cross... he suffereth together with us, as if the enemies of the gospel should wound us through his side."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul fell to the earth, overpowered, and heard the voice — "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" The doubled name (in the Hebrew form) fixed his attention and claimed him as a Hebrew called by God (Barnes, Ellicott). The question revealed the great truth of the mystic union: Christ and his people are one, so that to persecute the church is to persecute Christ himself (Barnes). Calvin draws the comfort: the Son of God is partner with his people in the cross, suffering with them, wounded through their side — and so he will avenge their wrongs. The verse displays both the humbling of Saul (struck to the earth) and the heart of the gospel: Christ so identified with his members that their sufferings are his. The persecutor learns that in hunting the disciples he has been warring against the risen Lord himself. The mystic union, here revealed to Saul, would become a cornerstone of his theology (the body of Christ); and the comfort that Christ suffers with his people sustains the persecuted church in every age. The Head feels the wounds dealt to his members.
5:
"And he said, Who art thou, Lord? And the Lord said, I am Jesus whom thou persecutest: it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks." The witnesses note Saul's question, Christ's self-disclosure, and a textual matter. On "Lord," the grammatical voices divide: Barnes and Robertson take it here as "Sir" (Saul not yet knowing the speaker), while Clarke and Ellicott see a dawning higher sense. Robertson: "Saul surrendered instantly... This surrender of the will to Christ was the conversion of Saul."

On "I am Jesus," the witnesses note the humbling. Barnes: "This declaration was fitted peculiarly to humble and mortify Saul... Jesus here uses that very designation: I am Jesus the Nazarene, the object of your contempt and scorn." Ellicott: the very Name "which he had been compelling men and women to blaspheme" now revealed as sharing the glory of the Father.

On "it is hard to kick against the pricks," the witnesses note it is absent from the best MSS here, inserted from Acts 26:14. Robertson: "The best MSS. do not have these words... The Textus Receptus put these words in here without the authority of a Greek codex." Clarke and Ellicott agree. On its meaning, the witnesses explain the proverb (common in Greek poets) of an ox kicking against the goad, only wounding itself. Ellicott applies the "pricks" to the warnings Saul had resisted — Stephen's face and prayer, Gamaliel's counsel, the misgivings of conscience.

The witnesses agree: Saul asked "Who art thou, Lord?" (whether "Sir" in dawning awe, Barnes, Robertson, or a higher sense, Clarke) and received the humbling answer, "I am Jesus" — the despised Nazarene he had persecuted, now revealed in glory (Barnes, Ellicott). The proverb "it is hard to kick against the pricks," though absent from the best MSS here (inserted from 26:14, Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott), belongs to the crisis: the ox wounds itself kicking the goad, as Saul had wounded himself resisting the "pricks" of conscience — Stephen's prayer, Gamaliel's counsel, the witness of the martyrs (Ellicott). The verse displays the decisive moment: the persecutor learns that the one he persecuted is the risen Lord, and that his resistance has been a vain and self-destructive fighting against God. Saul's surrender of his will to Christ in this moment was his conversion (Robertson). The proverb teaches the folly and peril of resisting a power infinitely superior — and the wisdom of yielding to the gracious will that was seeking to win him. The Nazarene he despised is the Lord of glory; and Saul, struck down, surrenders.
6:
"And he trembling and astonished said, Lord, what wilt thou have me to do? And the Lord said unto him, Arise, and go into the city, and it shall be told thee what thou must do." The witnesses note Saul's surrender and Christ's direction to human means. The grammatical voices note these words too are textually doubtful here (Robertson: "The best MSS. do not have... What wilt thou have me to do, Lord?"), drawn from the parallel accounts. Yet the substance belongs to the conversion.

On the surrender, Barnes draws the marks of true conversion: "Just before he had sought only to do his own will; now he inquired what was the will of the Saviour... This indicates the true nature of conversion. It is decided, prompt, immediate. Paul did not debate the matter (Galatians 1:16)." The first act of the converted soul is to give the will to Christ.

On "it shall be told thee," the Reformed Calvin draws out the great lesson that God uses human ministry: "whereas Paul is sent to Ananias, let us know that that is done to adorn the ministry of the Church... if Christ made Paul subject to the teaching of a common disciple, which of us can grudge to hear any teacher?" God could have taught Saul directly, but sends him to a man, that the ministry might be honoured. Barnes adds: even those converted in the most striking manner need the counsel of the church.

The witnesses agree: Saul, trembling and astonished, surrendered — "Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?" — the mark of true conversion, the prompt and entire giving of the will to Christ (Barnes), though the words are textually uncertain here (Robertson). And Christ directed him not to immediate revelation but to human means: "go into the city, and it shall be told thee." Calvin draws the great lesson: God sends Saul to Ananias, a common disciple, to adorn the ministry of the church — for if the chief apostle was subject to a humble teacher, none may grudge to be taught by God's ministers. The verse displays both the nature of conversion (the will surrendered, the proud persecutor now asking Christ's will) and the honour God puts on human ministry (the converted Saul dependent on a disciple in Damascus for direction). Even the most extraordinary conversion does not exempt from the counsel of the church (Barnes). The persecutor, struck down and surrendered, is led blind into the city to be taught by one of the very disciples he came to bind — the wolf made a lamb, sent to the shepherd.
7:
"And the men which journeyed with him stood speechless, hearing a voice, but seeing no man." The witnesses address the apparent contradiction with Acts 22:9, where Paul says the men "heard not the voice." The grammatical voices resolve it as a distinction between sound and articulate words. Barnes: "they heard a sound, they were amazed and alarmed, but they did not hear the distinct words addressed to Saul." He cites the parallel of the voice from heaven heard as thunder (John 12:28-29).

Clarke draws the same distinction: "they heard the thunder which followed the escape of the lightning, but they heard not the voice of him that spake to Saul; they did not hear the words." Robertson notes the grammatical basis (the case of the verb "hear"): "They heard the sound (9:7), but did not understand the words (22:9)," though he allows the distinction is possible rather than necessary.

The Reformed Calvin gives the same resolution: "it may be that they heard the sound of the voice, yet did they not discern either who it was that spake, or what was spoken... Paul alone is taught what he must do." The companions were witnesses to the reality of the event (the light, the voice, Saul prostrate), yet the message was for Saul alone.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul's companions stood speechless, hearing a voice but seeing no man — and the apparent contradiction with 22:9 ("heard not the voice") is resolved by the distinction between hearing a sound and understanding articulate words. They heard the sound but did not distinguish the message addressed to Saul alone (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Calvin), as the crowd heard the voice from heaven as thunder (John 12:29). The companions were genuine witnesses to the reality of the event — the light, the prostrate Saul, the sound from heaven — yet the message was Saul's alone. The verse displays both the objective reality of the conversion (witnessed by the companions) and its personal character (the words spoken to Saul only). The variations among the three accounts, far from discrediting them, reflect the natural difference between a manifestation partial to some and complete to one (Ellicott, under v.3). The companions saw the light and heard the sound; Saul alone saw the Lord and heard his words. The reality is attested; the call is personal — the pattern of every true conversion, witnessed by others yet wrought in the individual soul.
8:
"And Saul arose from the earth; and when his eyes were opened, he saw no man: but they led him by the hand, and brought him into Damascus." The witnesses note Saul's blindness and his helpless entry into the city. On the cause, Barnes holds it the natural effect of the intense light: "There is no reason to suppose that there was a miracle in this blindness, for in Acts 22:11 it is expressly said to have been caused by the intense light." The cure, however, was miraculous (v.18).

Ellicott notes the blindness "of one who has been dazzled with excess of light," and traces its possible permanent effects: Paul's dictating rather than writing (the "large characters" of Galatians 6:11), and perhaps the "thorn in the flesh" (2 Corinthians 12:7) as an affection of the eyes. He draws the spiritual significance: "He had looked on himself as a guide of the blind... Now, for a time, he had to accept his blindness."

The Reformed Calvin draws the spiritual lesson: "he is deprived of the sight of his body three days, that he may begin to see with his mind; for those must become fools, whosoever they be, which seem to themselves wise, that they may attain to true wisdom." The proud persecutor, who boasted his learning, is led helpless by the hand.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul arose blind — the natural effect of the intense light (Barnes, 22:11), though the cure would be miraculous (v.18) — and was led helpless by the hand into Damascus. Ellicott traces the possible permanent effects on Paul's sight (the large characters, the "thorn in the flesh") and the spiritual significance: the self-styled guide of the blind must now accept his own blindness. Calvin draws the lesson: the proud, learned persecutor is made blind that he may begin to see with his mind — the wise must become fools to attain true wisdom. The verse displays the humbling of Saul: the man who set out breathing slaughter, armed with authority, now enters Damascus blind and helpless, led by the hand like a child (Robertson). The masterful persecutor is reduced to dependence — a fitting prelude to the new birth (v.9). The blindness was both the natural effect of the vision and a spiritual parable: the one who thought he saw was blind, and must be given new sight by the Christ he had persecuted. Led helpless into the city he came to ravage, Saul awaits the disciple who will open his eyes.
9:
"And he was three days without sight, and neither did eat nor drink." The witnesses note the three days of blindness and fasting as the time of Saul's new birth. Barnes attributes the fasting to overwhelming grief and conviction: "he was overwhelmed with a view of his sins... His past life; his great sins; the sudden change in his views; his total absorption in the vision... would all fill his mind, and indispose him to partake of food." He had yet no assurance of forgiveness, but was "brought to the dust, and left to three painful days of darkness and suspense."

Ellicott reads the three days as a time of spiritual communion: "It is natural to think of this period of seclusion from the visible world as one of spiritual communion with the invisible... the visions and revelations of the Lord, the soaring as to the third heaven (2 Corinthians 12:1-4)." The conditions of outward life were suspended, and Saul lived "in the ecstacy of an apocalyptic rapture."

The Reformed Calvin notes the fast as part of the miracle and a sign of his terror: "we gather that Paul was wonderfully afraid, seeing that being, as it were, dead, he tasted no meat for three days." Wesley calls it "an important season! So long he seems to have been in the pangs of the new birth."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul passed three days blind, neither eating nor drinking — the time of his new birth (Wesley). Barnes attributes the fast to overwhelming conviction of sin and the absence of assurance, left in darkness and suspense; Ellicott reads it as a time of spiritual communion, perhaps the visions and revelations of the Lord (2 Corinthians 12); Calvin sees it as part of the miracle and a sign of his terror. The verse displays the depth of Saul's humbling and the travail of his conversion: the proud persecutor, struck blind, lies in darkness, fasting, overwhelmed with the sight of his sins and awaiting he knows not what. These three days of suspense and seclusion were the womb of the new man — the old life ended, the new not yet begun, the soul brought to the dust before God. The Saul who would arise (v.18) baptized and filled with the Spirit was being formed in these three dark days. The verse marks the pangs of the new birth: the violent persecutor brought to silence, blindness, and fasting, that he might be raised a new man in Christ. From this darkness Ananias would call him to light and life.
10:
"And there was a certain disciple at Damascus, named Ananias; and to him said the Lord in a vision, Ananias. And he said, Behold, I am here, Lord." The witnesses note the calling of Ananias to be the instrument of Saul's restoration. Chrysostom marks the providence: God prepares both men by vision — "While God prepares Ananias by a vision to go and minister to Saul, he at the same time prepares Saul by another vision to profit by this ministry."

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the humility of the instrument. Ananias was a private disciple, not an apostle, yet God chose him to open the eyes of the future chief apostle and to fill him with the Spirit — a striking honour put on the ordinary ministry of the church. His ready answer, "Behold, I am here, Lord," marks his obedience, like Samuel's (1 Samuel 3:4).

The coincidence of the two visions the witnesses note as the providence of God. The Lord prepares Ananias to go and Saul to receive him — the converging of means that displays the divine design (Ellicott, under v.12). The verse opens the account of how the converted Saul was brought into the fellowship and ministry of the church through a humble disciple.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which introduces Ananias. The witnesses agree: the Lord called Ananias, a disciple at Damascus, in a vision, and he answered, "Behold, I am here, Lord" — the ready obedience of a servant (like Samuel, 1 Samuel 3:4). Chrysostom marks the converging providence: God prepares Ananias by vision to go, and Saul by vision to receive him. The choice of a humble, private disciple — not an apostle — to restore the sight of the future chief apostle and fill him with the Spirit displays the honour God puts on the ordinary ministry of the church (Calvin, under v.6). The verse opens the account of Saul's reception into the church: the converted persecutor brought, not by direct revelation alone, but through the ministry of a brother. The coincidence of the two visions shows the unity of God's design, converging means toward a single gracious purpose. Ananias, ready and obedient, is the instrument by which the blind Saul will be brought to light, baptism, and the Spirit — the church receiving its former persecutor through one of its humblest members. The verse displays the providence of God in conversion: he prepares both the messenger and the one to be reached, that his purpose may be fulfilled through human means.
11:
"And the Lord said unto him, Arise, and go into the street which is called Straight, and inquire in the house of Judas for one called Saul, of Tarsus: for, behold, he prayeth," The witnesses note the precise direction and the great sign of Saul's conversion: "behold, he prayeth." On the particularity, Clarke: "How very particular is this direction! And it was necessary that it should be so, that he might see the whole to be a Divine communication." The street, the house, the name, the city of origin — all are given.

On "behold, he prayeth," Barnes draws out the mark of conversion: "This gives us a full indication of the manner in which Saul passed the three days... his change is indicated by his giving himself to prayer. Before he was a persecutor; now... the prayers of a broken-hearted sinner." He notes the contrast: before, the prayers of a Pharisee depending on his own righteousness; now, the prayers of a sinner depending on the mercy of God in the Messiah.

Barnes draws the general lesson: "It is a sufficient indication of the character of any man to say, Behold, he prays... Knowing this, we know all about him." Real prayer — humble, sincere, dependent on God's mercy — is the surest mark of the converted soul. The praying Saul is no longer the persecutor but the penitent.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Lord directed Ananias with precise particularity (the street, house, name, and city — all given, that he might know it a divine communication, Clarke) to Saul of Tarsus, "for, behold, he prayeth." This is the great sign of Saul's conversion: the man who breathed slaughter now prays — not the proud prayers of the Pharisee depending on his own righteousness, but the broken prayers of a penitent sinner depending on God's mercy (Barnes). Barnes draws the lesson: to say "behold, he prays" is sufficient indication of a man's character; real prayer is the surest mark of the converted soul. The verse displays the transformation of Saul: the persecutor become a penitent, the proud Pharisee become a humble suppliant, passing his three days of blindness in prayer. The Lord points Ananias to this as the assurance of the change: the one he feared as a persecutor is now found praying. The praying Saul is a new man — and prayer, the breath of the new life, is the token by which his conversion is known. The persecutor's rage has given way to the penitent's prayer.
12:
"And hath seen in a vision a man named Ananias coming in, and putting his hand on him, that he might receive his sight." The witnesses note the parallel vision given to Saul, preparing him to receive Ananias. Clarke: "While God prepares Ananias, by a vision, to go and minister to Saul, he at the same time prepares Saul, by another vision, to profit by this ministry." The coincidence of the two visions displays the providence of God.

Ellicott addresses the skeptical objection: "The coincidence of the two visions has seemed to some critics... to betray something like the skill of the artistic historian. To those who reject the supernatural altogether, this may seem a short and easy explanation. To those who have not brought themselves to that point of denial, it will not seem strange that there should be in the work of the highest Designer the same unity of purpose and convergence of varied means which rouse our admiration in works of human skill."

On Ananias laying hands "that he might receive his sight," Lightfoot raises the question of how a private disciple could confer the Spirit (v.17), this seeming the apostles' prerogative. The witnesses note that Ananias was the chosen instrument for this specific commission, directly authorized by the Lord.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul had been shown in a vision the coming of Ananias to restore his sight — the parallel vision preparing him to receive the messenger (Clarke). The coincidence of the two visions displays the providence of God, the convergence of means toward a single purpose; and Ellicott answers the skeptical objection that this betrays the "artistic historian" — to those who do not reject the supernatural, such unity of design in God's work is no stranger than in works of human skill. Lightfoot raises the question of how a private disciple could confer the Spirit (v.17), noting it seemed the apostles' prerogative, yet Ananias was directly authorized for this commission. The verse displays the gracious preparation of Saul: the Lord, having sent Ananias, has also shown Saul his coming, that the meeting may be received in faith. The two visions, converging, are the signature of divine design — God preparing both the messenger and the one to be reached. Saul, blind and praying, awaits the disciple he has been shown; Ananias, instructed and assured, goes to the persecutor he has feared. The providence that arrested Saul now brings him into the fellowship of the church through a humble brother divinely prepared.
13:
"Then Ananias answered, Lord, I have heard by many of this man, how much evil he hath done to thy saints at Jerusalem:" The witnesses note Ananias's hesitation and the first use of "saints" for the disciples. On the hesitation, Barnes reads it charitably: "the passage of such a train of thoughts through the mind was perfectly natural at the command to go and search out Saul. There would instantly occur all that had been heard of his fury in persecution." Yet "such thoughts should have been banished, and he should have gone at once."

The grammatical voices note the first use of "saints." Ellicott: "This is noticeable as the first application of the term saints to the disciples. The primary idea of the word was that of men who consecrated themselves, and led a devout life." He traces it to the Chasidim (the devout of the Maccabean period) and notes it became prominent in Paul's own writings — passing, significantly, "from Ananias to St. Paul." Robertson: "Ananias in his ignorance saw in Saul only the man with an evil reputation while Jesus saw in Saul the man transformed by grace."

The Reformed Calvin reads the hesitation as weakness of faith mixed with obedience: "In that Ananias objecteth the danger to the Lord, he betrayeth his weakness of faith therein... yet this is a point of a good man, that he yieldeth not so much to fear that he withdraweth himself from Christ's obedience." His was "a desire to obey mixed with fear."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Ananias hesitated, objecting that he had heard of the evil Saul had done to "thy saints at Jerusalem." The hesitation was natural (the memory of Saul's fury, Barnes) yet a weakness of faith mixed with obedience (Calvin) — a desire to obey tempered by fear. The verse marks the first use of "saints" for the disciples — the devout, the consecrated — a term that, significantly, passed from Ananias to Paul and became prominent in his epistles (Ellicott). Robertson notes the contrast: Ananias saw only the persecutor's evil reputation, while Jesus saw the man transformed by grace. The verse displays the human reluctance to believe in the conversion of a great enemy, and the gentleness of God in dealing with his servant's fear. Ananias's hesitation, though a weakness, did not become disobedience: at the Lord's second word (v.15) he went. The verse also marks the church's naming of its members as "saints" — set apart and consecrated to God — a term the converted Saul would make his own. The disciple's fear is met not with rebuke but with assurance, and the persecutor's transformation is affirmed by the Lord who knows the heart.
14:
"And here he hath authority from the chief priests to bind all that call on thy name." The witnesses note Ananias's further objection and the description of Christians as "those who call on thy name." On the authority, the grammatical voices note Ananias had likely been warned by letters from Jerusalem of Saul's coming commission (Robertson). His objection presses the danger: Saul came armed with authority to bind the disciples.

On "all that call on thy name," Ellicott traces the growth of terminology: "The description of the disciples of the Lord Jesus as those who called upon or invoked His name, had its origin in the words of Joel cited by St. Peter (Acts 2:21), and afterwards by St. Paul (Romans 10:13)." He notes the same word is used of calling on the Father (1 Peter 1:17) and on Christ — and that this term too passed from Ananias to Paul.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of Christ's divinity from the phrase: "invocation cannot be without sure confidence... by both which the divinity of Christ is not only proved... we are taught by the example of the faithful, to call upon the name of Christ when he is preached to us." To call on Christ's name is to worship him as God, placing all hope of salvation in him.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Ananias pressed his objection — Saul had authority from the chief priests to bind "all that call on thy name." The verse marks another term for the disciples: those who call on (invoke) the name of Christ, originating in Joel's prophecy cited by Peter (2:21) and Paul (Romans 10:13), and passing, like "saints," from Ananias to Paul (Ellicott). Calvin draws the doctrine: to call on Christ's name is to worship him as God, placing all hope of salvation in him — a proof of his divinity, for invocation rests on sure confidence. The verse displays both Ananias's lingering fear (Saul armed to bind the disciples) and the church's confession: Christians are those who call on the name of the Lord Jesus, invoking him as God and Savior. The very disciples Saul came to bind are defined by their worship of the Christ he persecuted — and that worship, addressed to Jesus, attests his deity. The persecutor sought to silence those who called on the name; he would soon call on it himself (22:16) and proclaim it to the nations. The name Saul came to suppress is the name in which the church's salvation rests.
15:
"But the Lord said unto him, Go thy way: for he is a chosen vessel unto me, to bear my name before the Gentiles, and kings, and the children of Israel:" The witnesses note the Lord's overruling of Ananias's fear and the great commission of Saul. On "go thy way," Barnes: "This is often the only answer that we obtain to the suggestion of our doubts... God tells us still to do what he requires, with an assurance only that his commands are just."

On "a chosen vessel" (literally, a vessel of election), the witnesses note the election language. Ellicott: "The word election, which occurs here for the first time in the New Testament, and is afterwards so prominent in the teaching of St. Paul (Romans 9:11; 11:5)... affords yet another instance of the influence exercised on the Apostle by the thoughts and language of the instructor." Robertson: "Jesus chose Saul before Saul chose Jesus." Wesley: "It is undeniable, that some men are unconditionally chosen or elected, to do some works for God." Ellicott suggests the "vessel" may contain the germ of the potter's-vessel image of Romans 9.

On the threefold scope — Gentiles, kings, and Israel — Barnes notes the fulfillment: the Gentiles (Paul's chief work), kings (before Agrippa and Nero), and Israel (to whom he preached first in every place). The chief element was the apostleship to the Gentiles (Robertson).

The witnesses agree: the Lord overruled Ananias's fear — "Go thy way" — and revealed Saul's great commission: "a chosen vessel" (a vessel of election) to bear Christ's name before the Gentiles, kings, and Israel. The verse marks the first NT use of "election," a term that became prominent in Paul's own teaching (Romans 9, 11) — an instance, Ellicott notes, of the influence of Ananias's language on the apostle; Robertson: "Jesus chose Saul before Saul chose Jesus"; Wesley sees here unconditional election to a work. The "vessel" may contain the germ of the potter's-vessel image (Ellicott). The threefold scope was fulfilled: the Gentiles (his chief work), kings (Agrippa, Nero), and Israel (Barnes). The verse displays the sovereign grace that chose the persecutor to be the apostle: the man who breathed slaughter is the vessel of election to bear Christ's name to the nations. God's answer to Ananias's fear is the revelation of his eternal purpose — Saul chosen, before the foundation, for this very work. The persecutor of the name becomes its chief herald; the enemy of the church becomes the apostle to the Gentiles, chosen and equipped by the sovereign will of God.
16:
"For I will shew him how great things he must suffer for my name's sake." The witnesses note that suffering was part of Saul's call. Barnes: "He would not merely profess repentance, but would manifest the sincerity of it by encountering trials and reproaches for his sake. The prediction here was fully accomplished (Acts 20:23; 2 Corinthians 11:23-27)." The persecutor would become the sufferer.

Clarke draws the contrast: "Instead of proceeding as a persecutor, and inflicting sufferings on others, I will show him how many things he himself must suffer for preaching that very doctrine which he has been hitherto employed in persecuting. Strange change indeed!" The man who made others suffer would now suffer for the name.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on the cross and the ministry: "I will accustom him to suffer troubles, to endure reproaches, and to abide all manner of conflicts... no man is fit to preach the gospel, seeing the world is set against it, save only he which is armed to suffer." Christ himself becomes Saul's teacher in suffering; and the minister must seek not only knowledge but constancy and strength.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Lord declared that suffering was part of Saul's call — "how great things he must suffer for my name's sake" — fully accomplished in his life of trials (Barnes, 2 Corinthians 11). Clarke marks the strange change: the persecutor who inflicted suffering would now suffer for the very doctrine he persecuted. Calvin draws the lesson: Christ accustoms Saul to bear the cross, and no man is fit to preach the gospel, against which the world is set, save one armed to suffer. The verse displays the cost of the apostleship: the chosen vessel is also the suffering servant, called not to ease but to endure great things for the name. The prospect of suffering, which deters lower characters, was welcome to the penitent Saul as a way to manifest his repentance (Ellicott) — to suffer for the name he had caused others to suffer for. The verse marks suffering as integral to the call of Christ's servants: to bear his name is to bear his cross. The persecutor becomes the sufferer; the one who made havoc of the church becomes the chief of its martyrs in labor and endurance. To be a vessel of election is to be a vessel for suffering — for the name's sake.
17:
"And Ananias went his way, and entered into the house; and putting his hands on him said, Brother Saul, the Lord, even Jesus, that appeared unto thee in the way as thou camest, hath sent me, that thou mightest receive thy sight, and be filled with the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note Ananias's obedience and his gracious greeting. Gill: "he arose from off his bed... went out of his house... he had nothing more to say, his objections were answered; there was no room to excuse himself... he quietly yields, and cheerfully obeys."

The witnesses dwell on the word "Brother Saul." The disciple who had feared the persecutor now greets him as a brother — the church receiving its former enemy with love. Ananias names the Lord Jesus who appeared to Saul, confirming the reality of the vision (the appearance attesting the resurrection, Clarke, under v.7), and declares the twofold purpose: the restoration of sight and the filling with the Spirit.

Cyril of Jerusalem dwells on the filling with the Spirit: "the Spirit's mighty working changed the blindness of Paul's eyes into newness of sight; and having vouchsafed His seal unto his soul, made him a chosen vessel to bear the Name of the Lord." The laying on of hands, by a private disciple, conveyed both sight and the Spirit — the commission directly authorized by the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Ananias, his objections answered, obeyed cheerfully (Gill), entered the house, laid hands on Saul, and greeted him — "Brother Saul." The disciple who had feared the persecutor now receives him as a brother, the church embracing its former enemy with love. Ananias names the Lord Jesus who appeared in the way (confirming the vision and the resurrection) and declares the twofold purpose: the restoration of sight and the filling with the Holy Spirit (Cyril). The verse displays the gracious reception of Saul into the fellowship of the church: not by an apostle but by a humble disciple, who calls the former persecutor "brother" and lays hands on him for sight and the Spirit. The greeting "Brother Saul" is the seal of his acceptance — the wolf received as a sheep, the enemy as a brother. The laying on of hands, conveying both healing and the Spirit, marks the completion of Saul's conversion: blind no longer, filled with the Spirit, received into the church. The man who came to bind the disciples is embraced by one of them as a brother in Christ — the grace of the gospel made manifest in a single word.
18:
"And immediately there fell from his eyes as it had been scales: and he received sight forthwith, and arose, and was baptized." The witnesses note the restoration of sight and Saul's baptism — and divide on the "scales." Barnes stresses the qualifying "as it had been": "It is not said that scales literally fell from his eyes; but that an effect followed as if scales had been suddenly taken off... no such literal removing would restore the sight." The cure was miraculous (sudden, by Ananias's hand), though the blindness had a natural cause.

Others read a literal incrustation. Clarke: "This was real... the structure of the cornea was doubtless much disturbed... In the miraculous cure the membrane was restored to its primitive state, and the opaque matter separated from the cornea, in the form of thin laminae or scales." Ellicott: "an incrustation, caused by acute inflammation," comparing the "whiteness" that peeled from Tobit's eyes (Tobit 11:13) and noting Luke's medical precision.

On the baptism, Ellicott notes it the indispensable condition of admission to the church: "No visions and revelations of the Lord, no intensity of personal conversion, exempted him from it. For him, too, that was the washing of regeneration." The witnesses note it was administered by Ananias, probably in one of the Damascus rivers.

The witnesses agree: immediately Saul's sight was restored — something "as it had been scales" falling from his eyes — and he arose and was baptized. They divide on the scales: Barnes stresses the qualifying "as it had been," denying a literal removal (the cure being miraculous in its suddenness, though the blindness had a natural cause); Clarke and Ellicott read a literal incrustation peeling off, noting Luke's medical precision (the parallel of Tobit 11:13). The verse displays the completion of the miracle: the blind persecutor receives sight by the hand of a humble disciple, and at once submits to baptism — the indispensable condition of admission to the church, the washing of regeneration (Ellicott), from which no vision or conversion exempted him. The verse marks the public seal of Saul's new life: cleansed, enlightened, baptized, he is now a member of the body he had persecuted. The scales falling from his eyes are both the cure of his blindness and a parable of the removal of his spiritual blindness — the veil taken away that he might see Christ. Baptized by Ananias, the former enemy enters the visible church, and the new man, filled with the Spirit, is ready to proclaim the name he once sought to destroy.
19:
"And when he had received meat, he was strengthened. Then was Saul certain days with the disciples which were at Damascus." The witnesses note Saul's recovery and his fellowship with the disciples. On the strengthening, the grammatical voices note that the three days' fast had brought extreme prostration; food restored his bodily strength after his soul had received strength (Clarke, Calvin, under v.18).

On "certain days with the disciples," Barnes notes the fellowship: the former persecutor now joined to the very disciples he came to bind. He also notes the journey to Arabia (Galatians 1:17), omitted by Luke: "This visit to Arabia Luke has omitted, but there is no contradiction." Ellicott places the Arabian journey here, after the "certain days," as a time of seclusion and preparation before the public preaching.

Barnes uses this verse for an extended argument on the evidential value of Saul's conversion: "This conversion has always been justly considered as a strong proof of the Christian religion." He gives the reasons — Saul had no worldly motive (Christians were poor and persecuted), he was a bitter enemy before, his subsequent life of suffering proved his sincerity, and his testimony as a former persecutor is unimpeachable: "If Paul was sincere — if this conversion was genuine — the Christian religion is true."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Saul, refreshed with food after his fast, was strengthened, and spent certain days with the disciples at Damascus — the former persecutor now in fellowship with the very disciples he came to bind. Luke omits the journey to Arabia (Galatians 1:17), which Ellicott places here as a time of seclusion and preparation. Barnes draws from the whole conversion its great evidential force: Saul, a bitter enemy with no worldly motive to change (Christians being poor and persecuted), was transformed and spent his life suffering for the faith — his testimony as a former persecutor unimpeachable, and his sincerity a strong proof of the truth of Christianity. The verse marks the beginning of Saul's life in the church: strengthened, in fellowship, soon to preach. The man who breathed slaughter now sits among the disciples as a brother. The verse closes the conversion narrative and opens his ministry: the chosen vessel, healed, baptized, strengthened, and received, is ready to bear the name of Christ. His conversion stands as one of the strongest evidences of the gospel — the enemy made an apostle, the persecutor a sufferer, by the power of the risen Christ he met on the road.
20:
"And straightway he preached Christ in the synagogues, that he is the Son of God." The witnesses note Saul's immediate preaching and its theme. On "straightway," Barnes: "Immediately. This was an evidence of the genuineness of his conversion, that he was willing at once to avow himself to be the friend of the Lord Jesus." The fruit of his conversion appeared at once in bold preaching.

On the text, the witnesses note the better reading "Jesus" rather than "Christ." Robertson: "Jesus, not Christ, is the correct text here." Barnes and Clarke agree: the question for the Jews was not whether the Christ would be the Son of God (which they granted), but whether Jesus was that Christ and Son of God. Saul preached "that this Jesus" — the crucified Nazarene — "is the Son of God."

On "the Son of God," Robertson notes this is Paul's platform: "Paul boldly calls Jesus the Son of God with full acknowledgment of his deity from the very start... With this faith he can shake the world." The Reformed Calvin notes the swiftness of the fruit: "he who of late ran headlong against Christ with furious force, doth now not only submit himself meekly... but like a stout standard-bearer fighteth even unto the utmost danger to maintain his glory."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul straightway preached in the synagogues that Jesus (the better reading, Robertson, Barnes, Clarke) is the Son of God — the immediate fruit of his conversion, proof of its genuineness (Barnes). The question for the Jews was not whether the Christ would be God's Son, but whether the crucified Jesus was that Christ and Son — and this Saul boldly proclaimed. Robertson notes this is Paul's lifelong platform: the full deity of Jesus, acknowledged from the start, with which he could shake the world. Calvin marks the swiftness: the man who lately ran against Christ now fights as his standard-bearer to maintain his glory. The verse displays the complete reversal: the persecutor become the preacher, in the very synagogues where he meant to arrest the disciples, proclaiming the deity of the Jesus he had persecuted. The boldness and immediacy of his preaching attest the reality of his conversion. From the first, Saul's gospel is the Son of God — Jesus the crucified Nazarene, risen and divine, the Christ of God. The chosen vessel begins at once to bear the name, turning the weapons of the persecutor against the camp he had served.
21:
"But all that heard him were amazed, and said; Is not this he that destroyed them which called on this name in Jerusalem, and came hither for that intent, that he might bring them bound unto the chief priests?" The witnesses note the amazement at Saul's change. The grammatical voices note the word "destroyed" (made havoc). Clarke explains its threefold force: Saul had treated the Christians as an enemy, imprisoned them, and slain them — "therefore these three meanings of the original word are all exemplified in the conduct of Saul." Robertson notes Paul uses the same verb of himself (Galatians 1:13,23).

The Reformed Calvin draws out the evidential value of the amazement: "This is added, that we may know that the power of God was acknowledged. For seeing that the zeal of Paul against the gospel was openly known, they saw no other cause of such a sudden change but the hand of God." The very astonishment of the hearers, who knew his former rage, testified to the divine power of his conversion.

The witnesses note again the phrase "those which call on this name" — the worship of Christ. Calvin: "whatsoever the Scripture commandeth concerning calling upon the name of God, it agreeth to the person of Christ." The hearers' amazement underscores both the reality of the change and the magnitude of what God had done.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: all who heard Saul were amazed — "Is not this he that destroyed" (made havoc of) "them which called on this name?" The verb's threefold force (treating as enemy, imprisoning, slaying) describes Saul's former conduct (Clarke), the same word he uses of himself (Galatians 1:13, Robertson). Calvin draws the evidential value: the amazement of those who knew his rage testified that no cause but the hand of God could explain so sudden a change — the very astonishment a proof of the divine power of his conversion. The verse displays the impact of the transformed Saul: the hearers, who knew him as the chief persecutor come to bind the disciples, are astonished to hear him preach the name he sought to destroy. Their amazement is itself a witness to the reality and divine origin of his conversion. The persecutor's change could not be explained by any human motive; it was manifestly the work of God. And the phrase "call on this name" again marks the church's worship of Christ as God. The very enemy who came to bind those who called on the name now calls on it himself and proclaims it — to the amazement of all who knew his former fury.
22:
"But Saul increased the more in strength, and confounded the Jews which dwelt at Damascus, proving that this is very Christ." The witnesses note the growth of Saul's power and his confuting of the Jews. On "increased in strength," Ellicott connects it to the spiritual power of his ministry, and to the vision of 2 Corinthians 12:9 ("My strength is made perfect in weakness"); the same verb Paul uses of himself ("I can do all things through Christ that strengtheneth me," Philippians 4:13). Robertson: "Christ, the dynamo of spiritual energy, was now pouring power into Paul."

On "confounded," the grammatical voices note it means to confute, to throw into confusion. Barnes: "Paul confuted the Jews, and by so doing he was the occasion of their tumultuous proceedings." The more he preached, the more the Jews were confounded — unable to answer his proof.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on the power of Scripture: "he got the victory in disputation... being furnished with testimonies of Scripture... he did, as it were, tread all his adversaries under his feet... Whence had Paul this victory, save only because the Scripture was his sword?" He notes the Jews were confounded yet not converted — overcome in argument but not yielding in heart.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Saul increased in strength — the spiritual power of Christ poured into him (Ellicott, Robertson, cf. Philippians 4:13) — and confounded (confuted) the Damascus Jews, proving from Scripture that Jesus is the Christ. Calvin draws the lesson: Saul's victory came because the Scripture was his sword, by which he trod his adversaries under his feet; yet the Jews were confounded without being converted — overcome in argument but not yielding in heart. The verse displays the growing power of the converted Saul: the more he preached, the stronger he grew and the more the Jews were confounded, unable to answer his scriptural proof that the crucified Jesus was the Messiah. The very arguments and learning he once used against the gospel were now turned to its defense — the most powerful artillery in the Jewish camp suddenly turned upon them (Robertson). The verse marks the irresistible force of Spirit-given preaching armed with Scripture: Saul confounds the Jews as Stephen had confounded the synagogues. Yet the confounding of argument does not by itself convert the heart — a reminder that the sword of the Word convinces, but the Spirit must subdue. Saul's power grew; the Jews' rage grew with it (v.23).
23:
"And after that many days were fulfilled, the Jews took counsel to kill him:" The witnesses note the Jews' plot against Saul. The puritan Henry and the grammatical voices note that "many days" covers the period including the Arabian journey (Galatians 1:17-18, about three years from the conversion). The Jews, confounded in argument (v.22) and unable to answer Saul's preaching, resort to violence.

The witnesses note the pattern: those who cannot overcome the truth by reason turn to murder. The Jews of Damascus, having lost the argument, plot to kill the preacher — the same resort to force that Saul himself had once used against the disciples (the irony noted by the witnesses). The plot fulfills Christ's word that Saul "must suffer" (v.16).

The plot the witnesses read as the beginning of Saul's long suffering for the name. Calvin (under v.25): "he was acquainted betime with the cross with this first exercise." The persecutor, now persecuted, begins to drink the cup he had made others drink — and learns, in his own escape, the meekness of the cross.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: after "many days" (covering the Arabian journey and about three years, Galatians 1, Henry), the Damascus Jews, confounded in argument, plotted to kill Saul — the resort to violence by those who cannot overcome the truth by reason. The plot fulfills Christ's word that Saul "must suffer" (v.16), and marks the beginning of his long suffering for the name. The verse displays the irony and the pattern: the former persecutor is now persecuted, the man who plotted against the disciples now the object of a plot. The Jews, unable to answer his preaching, sought to silence him by death — as Saul himself had once sought to silence the church. The persecutor learns the cross he had made others bear. The verse marks the transition from Saul's bold preaching at Damascus to his escape (vv.24-25) — the first of many deliverances God would grant him. The cup of suffering, foretold at his call, is now placed to his lips; and the chosen vessel begins to learn, by his own persecution, the fellowship of Christ's sufferings.
24:
"But their laying await was known of Saul. And they watched the gates day and night to kill him." The witnesses note the discovery of the plot and the watching of the gates. The grammatical voices connect this with Paul's own account (2 Corinthians 11:32): "In Damascus the governor under Aretas the king kept the city with a garrison, desirous to apprehend me." Ellicott explains the political situation: Damascus was under Aretas, and the ethnarch, wishing to court the Jewish population, took part in the plot, stationing troops at each gate.

The witnesses note the cooperation of the civil and the Jewish authorities. Barnes: "the Jews who were there might easily represent Saul as an offender, and demand his being secured." The gates were watched day and night, that Saul might not escape — the city sealed against the preacher.

The discovery of the plot the witnesses read as the providence of God. The plot "was known of Saul" — whether by revelation or by friends (Gill) — so that he might escape. God, who had appointed Saul to a long ministry, preserved him from this first attempt on his life. The watching of the gates would be foiled by the disciples' stratagem (v.25).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the plot was made known to Saul, and the Jews — with the cooperation of the ethnarch under Aretas (2 Corinthians 11:32, Ellicott) — watched the gates day and night to kill him. The city was sealed against the preacher, the civil and Jewish authorities joined against him (Barnes). Yet the discovery of the plot was the providence of God, preserving Saul (appointed to a long ministry) from this first attempt on his life. The verse displays both the danger and the deliverance: the gates watched, the preacher hunted, yet warned in time to escape. The God who had arrested Saul now guards him, foiling the watchers by the disciples' stratagem (v.25). The verse marks the reality of Saul's suffering for the name (v.16) — hunted in the very city of his conversion — and the faithfulness of God in preserving his chosen vessel. The persecutor, now persecuted, experiences the first of many deliverances; and the watching of the gates, intended to trap him, becomes the occasion of a famous escape. The plot known, the providence sure, Saul is preserved for the work God has given him.
25:
"Then the disciples took him by night, and let him down by the wall in a basket." The witnesses note Saul's escape. The grammatical voices connect it with Paul's own account (2 Corinthians 11:33): "through a window in a basket was I let down by the wall, and escaped." Robertson notes the escape was through a window in a house built on the wall, as in Rahab's rescue of the spies (Joshua 2:15) and David's escape (1 Samuel 19:12).

The witnesses note Paul's later reference to this escape as an "infirmity." Ellicott: "It seems to follow, from the tone in which the Apostle there speaks of this adventure, that it had been made matter of ridicule. It is connected in his thoughts with the infirmities of which he was content to boast." The undignified escape in a basket was a humbling memory.

The Reformed Calvin addresses the lawfulness of the escape: "it was no less lawful for the faithful to be let down in a basket, than it shall be lawful for any private person to leap over a wall, that he may avoid the sudden invasion of the enemy... the laws must always be inclined to equity." And he draws the lesson of God's humbling: "Paul is enforced to steal his life from the watchmen of the city... he reckoneth this example amongst his infirmities. He was acquainted betime with the cross with this first exercise."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples let Saul down by the wall in a basket by night (2 Corinthians 11:33), an escape like Rahab's spies (Joshua 2:15) and David's (1 Samuel 19:12, Robertson). Paul later refers to this undignified escape as an "infirmity" he was content to boast of — a humbling memory, perhaps made matter of ridicule (Ellicott). Calvin defends its lawfulness (avoiding the enemy by leaping the wall is no violation, the law inclining to equity) and draws the lesson of God's humbling: the apostle enforced to steal his life from the watchmen, acquainted betime with the cross. The verse displays both the deliverance and the humbling: the chosen vessel, hunted in Damascus, escapes ignominiously in a basket — preserved by God, yet brought low. The escape, far from a triumph, was a humiliation the apostle remembered among his infirmities — and so a lesson in the weakness through which God's strength is perfected (2 Corinthians 12:9). The persecutor, now persecuted, is delivered by the very disciples he came to bind, lowered over the wall by night. God preserves his servant, but by the path of humbling — the first exercise of the cross that would mark Saul's whole ministry.
26:
"And when Saul was come to Jerusalem, he assayed to join himself to the disciples: but they were all afraid of him, and believed not that he was a disciple." The witnesses note Saul's return to Jerusalem (about three years after his conversion, Galatians 1:18) and the disciples' fear. Chrysostom addresses the apparent discrepancy with Galatians 1:17 (where Paul says he went to Arabia and saw none of the apostles), offering several harmonizations — chiefly that Paul means he did not go to receive a commission from them, or that the visits are differently arranged.

On the disciples' fear, Barnes reads it sympathetically: "They remembered his former violence against Christians... If they had not heard of him during that time, they would naturally retain much of their old feelings towards him." He notes that confidence is a plant of slow growth, not springing up by sudden profession but by a course of trustworthy life.

The Reformed Calvin notes Saul's hard entrance and his humility: "These were yet hard entrances for Paul... All his own nation was set against him for Christ's cause. The Christians refuse him." Yet Saul "doth patiently suffer the brethren to refuse his company, seeing they had just cause of fear" — desiring only "that he may be numbered amongst the disciples of Christ," counting the lowest place among them more than all masterships in the synagogue.

The witnesses agree: Saul, come to Jerusalem (about three years after his conversion, Galatians 1:18), tried to join the disciples, but they feared him and disbelieved that he was a disciple. Chrysostom harmonizes the apparent discrepancy with Galatians 1:17 (Paul went not to receive a commission from the apostles). The disciples' fear was natural (the memory of his violence; confidence a plant of slow growth, Barnes), and Saul bore it patiently and humbly, desiring only to be numbered among Christ's disciples, counting the lowest place among them above all honors in the synagogue (Calvin). The verse displays the hard entrance of the convert: rejected by his own nation for Christ's sake and at first distrusted by the church, Saul might have been discouraged, yet waited meekly for God to reconcile the brethren to him. The verse marks the cost of his conversion — suspected on every side — and his humility in bearing it. The former persecutor, now seeking fellowship, is met with fear; but his patient submission (and Barnabas's testimony, v.27) would open the door. The man who came to bind the disciples now humbly seeks to be counted one of them — and the church, slow to trust, must learn that the wolf has become a lamb.
27:
"But Barnabas took him, and brought him to the apostles, and declared unto them how he had seen the Lord in the way, and that he had spoken to him, and how he had preached boldly at Damascus in the name of Jesus." The witnesses note Barnabas's gracious sponsorship of Saul. On Barnabas's readiness, Ellicott offers an explanation: "the Levite of Cyprus and the tent-maker had been friends in earlier years. The culture of which Tarsus was the seat would naturally attract a student from the neighbouring island." Robertson: "It is to the credit of Barnabas that he had the insight and the courage to stand by Saul at the crucial moment."

On "the apostles," the witnesses note (from Galatians 1:18-19) that Saul saw chiefly Peter and James the Lord's brother, the others probably being absent. Barnabas vouched for Saul, relating the three proofs: he had seen the Lord, the Lord had spoken to him, and he had preached boldly at Damascus.

The witnesses note the providential timing of Barnabas's intervention. Chrysostom (under v.26): "Barnabas — Son of Consolation is his appellation, whence also he makes himself easy of access to the man: for he was a kind man." The man whose name meant "consolation" became the bridge by which the church received its former persecutor. Barnabas's testimony relieved the disciples' fear.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Barnabas took Saul and brought him to the apostles (chiefly Peter and James, Galatians 1:18-19), vouching for him by the three proofs — he had seen the Lord, the Lord had spoken to him, and he had preached boldly at Damascus. Barnabas's readiness may be explained by an earlier friendship (the Cyprian and the Tarsian, Ellicott); his insight and courage in standing by Saul at the crucial moment are to his credit (Robertson). The "son of consolation" (Chrysostom) became the bridge by which the church received its former persecutor, relieving the disciples' fear. The verse displays the ministry of Barnabas: the kind and trusting disciple who, when others feared, took Saul by the hand and vouched for his conversion. His sponsorship opened the door of the church to the future apostle. The verse marks the importance of a trusting friend in the life of the convert: without Barnabas, Saul might have remained an outsider; through him, he was received into fellowship with the apostles. The "son of consolation" consoles the suspected convert and reconciles him to the church — a model of the generous faith that believes the best and bridges the gap between the doubting church and the misunderstood believer.
28:
"And he was with them coming in and going out at Jerusalem." The brief verse notes Saul's acceptance into the fellowship of the church. The grammatical voices note the idiom. Robertson: "Barnabas and Peter and James opened all the doors for Saul and the fear of the disciples vanished." Barnes: "he was admitted to their friendship, and recognised as a Christian and an apostle." The witnesses note (from Galatians 1:18) that this visit lasted fifteen days.

On "coming in and going out," the witnesses note the Hebraism for familiar fellowship. Calvin: "which speech signifieth amongst the Hebrews familiarity, as the inhabitants of cities are said to go in and out at the gates of the city." After Barnabas's testimony, Saul "began to be counted one of the flock, that he might be thoroughly known to the Church."

The acceptance of Saul the witnesses read as the fruit of Barnabas's sponsorship and the reality of his conversion. The fear of the disciples vanished; the former persecutor was received into free and familiar fellowship, coming and going among them as one of their own.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Saul was received into free and familiar fellowship — "coming in and going out" — a Hebraism for intimate association (Calvin), the doors opened by Barnabas, Peter, and James, and the disciples' fear vanished (Robertson). The visit lasted fifteen days (Galatians 1:18). The verse marks the full acceptance of Saul into the church: the former persecutor, vouched for by Barnabas, now counted one of the flock, associating freely with the apostles and disciples. The fear that met him (v.26) gave way to fellowship; the suspected convert became a trusted brother. The verse displays the completion of Saul's reception: no longer an outsider, he comes and goes among the disciples as one of their own. The man who came to bind them now walks among them in fellowship. The verse, brief as it is, marks a significant moment: the church's full embrace of its former enemy, made possible by Barnabas's testimony and the manifest reality of Saul's changed life. From this fellowship Saul would go forth (after the plot of v.29) to his work — but first, received and known, he tastes the communion of the saints he had once ravaged.
29:
"And he spake boldly in the name of the Lord Jesus, and disputed against the Grecians: but they went about to slay him." The witnesses note Saul's bold preaching to the Hellenists and the plot against his life. The grammatical voices note the "Grecians" (Hellenists) — the Greek-speaking Jews. Ellicott observes the significance: "it was as the leader of the Hellenistic Jews of the synagogue named in Acts 6:9 that Saul had first appeared in the history of the Church. Now... he sought to undo the evil that he had then wrought, by preaching to them the faith which he had then opposed."

Robertson notes the same point: Saul disputed in the very synagogues where he had once joined issue with Stephen, using the very arguments Stephen had used — "It was intolerable to these Hellenistic Jews now to hear Saul taking the place of Stephen." They offered Saul the same answer they gave Stephen: death.

The Reformed Calvin notes the boldness and the irony: "Behold, again, fury instead of zeal... But Paul, who of late ran up and down to vex the godly, can abide nowhere now. And yet this estate was far better for him, than if he should have reigned in peace." The persecutor, now persecuted by his former allies, finds his suffering a better estate than his former triumph.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Saul preached boldly and disputed against the Hellenists — the very synagogues (Acts 6:9) where he had once opposed Stephen — seeking to undo the evil he had wrought, taking Stephen's place and using his arguments (Ellicott, Robertson). The Hellenists, finding it intolerable, offered Saul the same answer they gave Stephen: death. Calvin notes the irony: the persecutor, now persecuted by his former allies, can abide nowhere, yet finds this suffering a better estate than his former triumph. The verse displays the boldness and the cost of Saul's ministry: he confronts the very men he once led, preaching the faith he once destroyed, and is marked for death as Stephen was. The man who consented to Stephen's death now stands in Stephen's place, facing the same fate. The verse marks the second plot against Saul's life (after Damascus) within a short time — the fulfillment of Christ's word that he must suffer (v.16). The persecutor become preacher, the ally become enemy of his former friends, Saul tastes the cup he made others drink. His boldness in the name he once blasphemed brings upon him the violence he once dealt.
30:
"Which when the brethren knew, they brought him down to Caesarea, and sent him forth to Tarsus." The witnesses note the brethren's care in sending Saul to safety. The grammatical voices note the route: from Jerusalem to Caesarea (on the coast), and thence to Tarsus, Saul's native city. Ellicott: "The fact that the brethren at Jerusalem took these measures for the Apostle's safety may be noted as a proof of their friendship."

On the return to Tarsus, the witnesses note both safety and ministry. Clarke: "This was his own city; and it was right that he should proclaim to his own countrymen and relatives that Gospel through which he was become wise to salvation." Ellicott notes that from Tarsus Saul did his evangelistic work in Cilicia (Galatians 1:21), where churches were already organized when later mentioned (Acts 15:41).

The witnesses note the providence and the parallel with the earlier escape. As at Damascus (v.25), so at Jerusalem, the brethren deliver Saul from a plot — God preserving his chosen vessel. Robertson imagines the scene at Tarsus: "this brilliant young rabbi, the pride of Gamaliel, returns home a preacher of the despised Jesus of Nazareth." The persecutor returns to his own city as a herald of the gospel.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the brethren, learning of the plot, brought Saul to Caesarea and sent him to Tarsus, his native city — a proof of their friendship (Ellicott) and the providence of God preserving him, as at Damascus. The return to Tarsus served both safety and ministry: there Saul would preach to his own countrymen and do his evangelistic work in Cilicia (Galatians 1:21; Acts 15:41, Clarke, Ellicott). The verse displays the care of the church for its threatened member and the providence of God: the chosen vessel, hunted in Jerusalem as in Damascus, is delivered and sent to his own city. Robertson pictures the homecoming: the brilliant rabbi, the pride of Gamaliel, returns a preacher of the despised Jesus. The verse marks the end of Saul's first visit to Jerusalem as a Christian and the beginning of his hidden years in Cilicia, where he would labor until Barnabas sought him out (Acts 11:25). The persecutor, twice delivered from death, returns to Tarsus to bear the name — the providence of God preserving him for the great work to come, and the love of the brethren guarding the one they had lately feared.
31:
"Then had the churches rest throughout all Judaea and Galilee and Samaria, and were edified; and walking in the fear of the Lord, and in the comfort of the Holy Ghost, were multiplied." The witnesses note the season of peace and growth that followed. On the cause of the rest, the witnesses note it was not merely Saul's conversion (which was three years past) but also his removal and, especially, the diversion of the Jews by a political crisis. Barnes and Clarke (following Lardner) explain: Caligula's order to set up his statue in the temple threw the Jews into consternation, occupying them so that they ceased to persecute the church. Barnes: "By the providence of God, this event was permitted to occur to divert the attention of bloody-minded persecutors from a feeble and a bleeding church."

On the marks of the church's health, the grammatical voices expound the rich description. The churches were "edified" (built up), "walking in the fear of the Lord" (reverent obedience), and "in the comfort of the Holy Ghost" (the consolation the Spirit produced) — and so "were multiplied." Clarke draws out the building metaphor at length: the soul rebuilt as a habitation for God.

Chrysostom (under v.26) connects the peace with Peter's resumption of travel: "while there was persecution, he was in Jerusalem, but when the affairs of the Church are everywhere in security, then it is that he leaves Jerusalem." The peace was the occasion for Peter's tour (vv.32ff.).

The witnesses agree: the churches throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had rest, were edified, and — walking in the fear of the Lord and the comfort of the Holy Spirit — were multiplied. The rest came not merely from Saul's conversion and removal but especially from the providential diversion of the Jews by Caligula's crisis (the order to set up his statue in the temple), which occupied them so that they ceased persecuting (Barnes, Clarke, Lardner). The marks of the church's health are richly given: built up, walking reverently, comforted by the Spirit, and growing. The verse displays the prosperity of the church in a season of peace: not relaxed by ease but edified, reverent, comforted, and multiplied. The fear of the Lord and the comfort of the Holy Spirit — reverence and consolation together — are the twin marks of a healthy church. The verse marks a turning point: the first great persecution, begun at Stephen's death and led by Saul, now ends; the church, having endured, enters a season of growth. The God who scattered the church in persecution now gives it rest, and the rest is fruitful. The peace was also the occasion for Peter's tour (vv.32ff.), in which Aeneas would be healed and Dorcas raised — the church at rest and the gospel advancing.
32:
"And it came to pass, as Peter passed throughout all quarters, he came down also to the saints which dwelt at Lydda." The witnesses note the resumption of the narrative with Peter's tour of the churches. Chrysostom (under v.26) pictures Peter "like the commander of an army, going about, inspecting the ranks, what part was compact, what in good order, what needed his presence." The peace of the church (v.31) freed Peter to visit and strengthen the scattered believers.

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note Peter's diligence and humility in this visitation. The chief of the apostles goes about among the churches, not lording it over them but serving and strengthening them — visiting even the lesser towns like Lydda. The term "saints" again describes the disciples (v.13).

The visitation the witnesses read as the pastoral care of the apostle. Peter, freed by the peace, travels to confirm and edify the churches — and in this tour two great miracles (the healing of Aeneas and the raising of Dorcas) would be wrought, advancing the gospel. The verse opens the account of Peter's ministry at Lydda and Joppa, which prepares for the great events of Caesarea (ch. 10).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which resumes the Petrine narrative. The witnesses agree: Peter, in a season of peace, went throughout the churches and came down to the saints at Lydda — like a commander inspecting his ranks (Chrysostom), in diligent and humble pastoral visitation (Henry). The peace of the church (v.31) freed him to confirm and strengthen the scattered believers. The verse opens the account of Peter's ministry at Lydda and Joppa, in which two great miracles would be wrought, advancing the gospel and preparing for the conversion of Cornelius (ch. 10). The verse displays the pastoral care of the apostle: Peter goes about among the churches, serving and strengthening them, visiting even the lesser towns. The "saints" at Lydda are the disciples there, consecrated to God. The verse marks the transition from the conversion of Saul to the ministry of Peter — the two great figures of the early church, the apostle to the Gentiles and the apostle to the circumcision, both now advancing the gospel. Peter's tour, beginning at Lydda, would lead through the raising of Dorcas at Joppa to the house of Cornelius, where the door of faith would be opened to the Gentiles. The peace of the church becomes the occasion of the gospel's advance.
33:
"And there he found a certain man named Aeneas, which had kept his bed eight years, and was sick of the palsy." The brief verse introduces Aeneas. The grammatical voices note the Greek name (probably a Hellenistic Jew, Barnes, Robertson) and the long, hopeless affliction. Robertson: "Luke the physician notes that he had been bed ridden for eight years." Clarke: "now inveterate and hopeless, through its long standing."
