The witnesses note the significance of the long affliction. As with the lame man at the Beautiful Gate (whose lameness was lifelong) and the man at Lydda's eight years of palsy, the chronic, hopeless nature of the disease magnified the miracle to come. No natural cure could be expected; the healing would be unmistakably divine.

On the name, Clarke notes the common practice of double names (Hebrew and Greek), as Aeneas may have been called Hananiah in Hebrew. Whether Aeneas was already a disciple (the "saints" of v.32) or not is debated, but the grammatical voices incline to his being a disciple.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter found at Lydda a man named Aeneas, bedridden eight years with palsy — a long, hopeless affliction noted by Luke the physician (Robertson). The chronic, incurable nature of the disease magnified the miracle to come: no natural cure could be expected, and the healing would be unmistakably divine. The Greek name suggests a Hellenistic Jew (Barnes), perhaps already a disciple among the "saints" (v.32). The verse introduces the first of Peter's two Lydda-and-Joppa miracles: the healing of a man whose eight years of palsy had made his case hopeless. As with the lifelong lameness healed at the Beautiful Gate, the long standing of the disease set off the power of the cure. The verse displays the providence that brought Peter to this man: in his pastoral tour, the apostle finds one whose healing would turn a whole region to the Lord (v.35). The hopeless case is the occasion for the manifestation of Christ's power through his servant. Aeneas, bedridden and beyond human help, awaits the word that will raise him — and through his healing the gospel will spread through Lydda and Sharon.
34:
"And Peter said unto him, Aeneas, Jesus Christ maketh thee whole: arise, and make thy bed. And he arose immediately." The witnesses note Peter's disclaimer of his own power and the immediate cure. On "Jesus Christ maketh thee whole," Barnes: "Peter claimed no power to do it himself," comparing Acts 3:6,16; 4:10. Clarke: "Not Peter, for he had no power but what was given him from above." The cure is ascribed wholly to Christ.

The grammatical voices note the assonance. Ellicott: "In the assonance of the Greek words (Iesus iatai se) we may perhaps trace a desire to impress the thought that the very name of Jesus testified that He was the great Healer." On "make thy bed," the witnesses note it as proof of the cure: he was to do at once for himself what others had done for him eight years (Clarke, Robertson).

The Reformed Calvin notes Peter's certainty and his role as minister: "Peter did not rashly break out into these words; because he might have set himself to be laughed at, unless he had already known the will of God... Peter showeth plainly that he is only the minister of the miracle, and that it proceedeth from the power of Christ; that he may by this means extol the name of Christ alone."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter, disclaiming his own power, declared "Jesus Christ maketh thee whole" — ascribing the cure wholly to Christ (Barnes, Clarke), the assonance of the Greek (Iesus iatai se) testifying that Jesus is the great Healer (Ellicott). The command "make thy bed" was proof of the cure: the man who had lain eight years now does for himself what others had done for him (Clarke, Robertson). Calvin notes Peter's certainty (he would not have spoken so unless he knew God's will) and his role as mere minister, extolling the name of Christ alone. The verse displays the apostolic miracle: Peter heals not by his own power but in the name of Jesus Christ, who is the true Healer. The immediate cure of a man bedridden eight years is unmistakably divine, and the bed made at once attests its reality. The verse models the apostolic preaching of the name: Peter, like at the Beautiful Gate (3:6), claims nothing for himself but gives all glory to Christ. The healing of Aeneas, ascribed wholly to Jesus, magnifies the name and turns the region to the Lord (v.35). The minister speaks; the Lord heals; the name is glorified.
35:
"And all that dwelt at Lydda and Saron saw him, and turned to the Lord." The witnesses note the wide effect of the miracle. The grammatical voices explain Saron (Sharon) as the fertile coastal plain, a district rather than a town (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). The healing of the well-known Aeneas was seen by all the region and led to a great turning to the Lord.

On "all," the witnesses note the common scriptural usage. Calvin: "when the Scripture saith all, it doth not comprehend every one... but it putteth all for the more part, or for many." The sense is that a great part of the people of Lydda and Sharon were converted.

On "turned to the Lord," the Reformed Calvin draws the proper end of miracles: "in this clause is expressed the fruit of the miracle, because they embraced Christ and his gospel. Wherefore those men corrupt miracles, whosoever they be, which look only upon men, and do not turn their eyes toward this end, that being instructed concerning the power and grace of Christ, they may stick only to him." The miracle's purpose was conversion.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: all who dwelt at Lydda and Sharon (the fertile coastal plain, a district, Barnes, Robertson) saw the healed Aeneas and turned to the Lord — "all" meaning a great part of the people (Calvin). The miracle, wrought on a well-known man, led to a wide conversion. Calvin draws the proper end of miracles: their purpose is to bring men to embrace Christ and his gospel, and those who look only at the wonder and not at this end corrupt the miracle. The verse displays the fruit of the healing: not merely the cure of one man but the turning of a whole region to the Lord. The miracle was a sign, pointing beyond itself to the power and grace of Christ, and its purpose was conversion. The verse marks the gospel's advance through Peter's ministry: the healing of Aeneas became the occasion of a great turning to the Lord throughout Lydda and Sharon. The sign confirmed the word; the power of Christ, displayed in the cure, drew many to faith. The miracle that glorified the name of Jesus (v.34) now gathers a harvest of souls — the proper and crowning fruit of every work of God: that men should turn to the Lord and stick only to him.
36:
"Now there was at Joppa a certain disciple named Tabitha, which by interpretation is called Dorcas: this woman was full of good works and almsdeeds which she did." The witnesses note the introduction of Dorcas, a model of charity. The grammatical voices note the two names (Tabitha in Aramaic, Dorcas in Greek, both meaning "gazelle"). The puritan Henry and the witnesses dwell on her character: "full of good works and almsdeeds" — a woman whose life was devoted to charity, especially to the poor widows (v.39).

Chrysostom (under v.26) notes the providence in her name: "to show that the name she bore matched her character; as active and wakeful was she as an antelope... She was full of good works: not only of alms, but of good works." Her diligence in charity matched the swift, watchful gazelle whose name she bore.

The witnesses note her place in the church's charity. She was, as it were, "at the head of a Sisterhood of Mercy" (Ellicott), making garments for the poor with the widows. Her good works were not mere almsgiving but the labor of her own hands — the coats and garments she made (v.39). Her life is the model of practical Christian charity.

The thin comment matches the verse, which introduces Dorcas. The witnesses agree: at Joppa was a disciple named Tabitha (Dorcas, "gazelle"), "full of good works and almsdeeds" — a woman whose life was devoted to charity, especially to the poor widows (Henry). Chrysostom notes the providence in her name: as active and watchful in good works as the swift gazelle she was named for. She was, as it were, at the head of a sisterhood of mercy, making garments for the poor with her own hands (Ellicott). The verse displays the model of practical Christian charity: not mere almsgiving but the diligent labor of love, clothing the needy and serving the widows. Dorcas's good works were the fruit of her faith, and her death would be felt as a great loss by those she served (v.39). The verse introduces the second of Peter's miracles — the raising of Dorcas — and sets before the church the pattern of a life full of good works. Her character, summed up in "full of good works and almsdeeds," is the highest commendation of practical piety. The disciple whose hands clothed the poor would be restored to life by the power of Christ, that her ministry might continue and the gospel be confirmed. Charity such as hers is the visible fruit of the faith that works by love.
37:
"And it came to pass in those days, that she was sick, and died: whom when they had washed, they laid her in an upper chamber." The witnesses note Dorcas's death and the preparation of her body. The grammatical voices note the washing of the dead (a widespread ancient custom, Clarke gives classical parallels) and the laying in the upper chamber. Barnes: "There is no evidence that they expected that Peter would raise her up to life." The careful preparation served, the witnesses note, to establish the reality of her death.

On the upper chamber, Ellicott notes the delay it implies: "This implies some little delay in the usual rapidity of Eastern funerals. As Lydda was only nine miles from Joppa, the report of Aeneas's recovery might well have travelled... and led to the hope that the power which St. Peter had thus put forth might extend even to the farther work of raising from the dead."

The Reformed Calvin reads the washing and the upper chamber as signs of some hope: "Whereas they carry her not straightway to the grave, but lay her in the upper part of the house, that they may keep her there, we may thereby gather that they had some hope of recovering her life." He gives an extended reflection on the ancient washing of the dead as a sign of the hope of the resurrection.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Dorcas fell sick and died, and her body was washed (the ancient custom, Clarke) and laid in an upper chamber — the careful preparation establishing the reality of her death. The laying in the upper chamber rather than immediate burial implies a delay and perhaps a hope (Ellicott, Calvin): the news of Aeneas's healing nearby may have raised the expectation that Peter's power might reach even to raising the dead. Calvin reflects on the ancient washing of the dead as a sign of the hope of the resurrection. The verse displays the loss felt at Dorcas's death and the lingering hope of her friends: they prepare her body but do not bury her, sending for Peter (v.38). The death of one so full of good works was a great loss to the church and the poor; yet the manner of the preparation suggests they did not abandon hope. The verse sets the stage for the raising of Dorcas: her death real and attested, her body prepared, her friends hoping in the apostle's power. The disciple whose hands clothed the poor lies dead, mourned by the widows she served — but the power of Christ, through his servant Peter, would restore her to life and continue her ministry of mercy.
38:
"And forasmuch as Lydda was nigh to Joppa, and the disciples had heard that Peter was there, they sent unto him two men, desiring him that he would not delay to come to them." The witnesses note the sending for Peter. The grammatical voices note the distance (Lydda about nine miles from Joppa) and the urgency of the message. Robertson: "Possibly the two messengers started before Dorcas was quite dead, though we do not know."

On their motive, the witnesses are cautious. Barnes: "Why they sent is not affirmed. It is probable that they desired his presence to comfort and sustain them in their affliction... but as the apostles had as yet raised no one from the dead... we have no authority for assuming that they had formed any such expectation." Clarke notes they may have sent before she died.

The Reformed Calvin reads a modest hope: "they humbly crave God's help, not that they will make Tabitha immortal, but their only desire is to have her life prolonged for a time, that she may yet profit the Church." Their hope, if any, was modest — that the useful Dorcas might be restored for the good of the church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples at Joppa, hearing Peter was nearby at Lydda (nine miles off), sent two men urging him not to delay. Their motive the witnesses read cautiously: perhaps comfort in affliction, perhaps a modest hope of her restoration (the apostles having raised no one yet, Barnes); possibly they sent before she died (Clarke, Robertson). Calvin reads a humble hope: not that Dorcas be made immortal, but that her useful life be prolonged for the church's good. The verse displays the disciples' resort to the apostle in their loss: whether for comfort or in hope, they send urgently for Peter. The death of so useful a disciple moved them to seek God's help through his servant. The verse marks the providence that brought Peter near at the hour of need: in his pastoral tour he was at Lydda, close enough to be summoned. The disciples' urgency ("not delay") reflects both their grief and their faith. The verse sets the stage for the raising of Dorcas: the apostle summoned, the church hoping, the dead disciple awaiting the power of Christ. Their modest hope — that the useful Dorcas might yet profit the church — would be answered beyond expectation in her restoration to life.
39:
"Then Peter arose and went with them. When he was come, they brought him into the upper chamber: and all the widows stood by him weeping, and shewing the coats and garments which Dorcas made, while she was with them." The witnesses note Peter's coming and the widows' grief. The grammatical voices note the touching scene. Robertson: "Vivid picture of this group of widows as they stood around Peter, weeping and showing the very inner garments and outer garments... which she made from time to time. It was a heart-breaking scene."

On the widows, Ellicott notes the church's organized charity: "the same organisation of charity as that which prevailed in the Church at Jerusalem. The widows of the Church were the object of a special provision... It is probable that the widows had been fellow-workers with her in making them. She was, as it were, at the head of a Sisterhood of Mercy." The garments were the visible memorial of her good works.

The Reformed Calvin notes the reason Dorcas was raised: "because God pitied the poor, and did at their desire restore the woman to life... God did respect the poor and widows in such sort, that, by relieving their poverty, he established in their minds the faith of his gospel." Her charity and the widows' need were the occasion of the miracle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter came and was brought to the upper chamber, where the widows stood weeping and showing the garments Dorcas had made — a heart-breaking scene (Robertson). The widows, objects of the church's organized charity and fellow-workers with Dorcas (Ellicott), displayed the coats and garments as the visible memorial of her good works and their loss. Calvin notes that God raised Dorcas because he pitied the poor and would, by relieving their need, establish their faith. The verse displays the fruit and the loss of a charitable life: the weeping widows, the garments she made, the grief of those she served. Her good works (v.36) are now seen in their effect — the poor clothed, the widows comforted, all now mourning her death. The verse marks the love that surrounded Dorcas and the void her death left in the church's ministry of mercy. The garments held up by weeping hands are the eloquent testimony of a life full of good works. Peter, moved by this scene, would put them forth and pray (v.40); and the God who pities the poor would restore their benefactress, that her ministry might continue and the gospel be confirmed. The widows' tears and the garments tell the worth of the disciple whom Christ would raise.
40:
"But Peter put them all forth, and kneeled down, and prayed; and turning him to the body said, Tabitha, arise. And she opened her eyes: and when she saw Peter, she sat up." The witnesses note Peter's method, prayer, and the raising of Dorcas. On putting them forth, the witnesses note the parallel with Christ raising Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:40), at which Peter was present. Barnes: "Perhaps because he did not wish to appear as if seeking publicity... Others suppose it was that he might offer more fervent prayer." Ellicott: "The work was one not to be accomplished by the mere utterance of a name... but by the power of the prayer of faith, and this called for the silence and solitude of communion with God."

On the prayer, the witnesses note Peter's dependence on God. Clarke: "during his wrestling with God, he had, undoubtedly, received confidence that she would be raised at his word." The kneeling and praying mark his humility and his reliance on Christ's power, not his own.

On "Tabitha, arise," Ellicott notes the near-echo of Christ's words: "the very words which were uttered, if he spoke in Aramaic, must have been, with the change of a single letter, the same as the Talitha cumi of Mark 5:41." Robertson notes the parallel with Elijah and Elisha (1 Kings 17:20; 2 Kings 4:33), who also prayed alone over the dead.

The witnesses agree: Peter put them all out (as Christ did with Jairus's daughter, Mark 5:40), kneeled and prayed, then said "Tabitha, arise," and she opened her eyes and sat up. The putting forth and the prayer mark both his desire to avoid ostentation and his need of fervent, solitary communion with God (Barnes, Ellicott): the raising was wrought not by the utterance of a name but by the prayer of faith, in dependence on Christ's power. The words "Tabitha, arise," if spoken in Aramaic, were almost the very Talitha cumi of Christ (Mark 5:41, Ellicott), and the scene echoes Elijah and Elisha praying alone over the dead (Robertson). The verse displays the apostolic raising of the dead: Peter, kneeling in prayer, dependent on Christ, speaks the word, and the dead disciple lives. Unlike Christ, who raised by his own authority, Peter raises by prayer — confessing that the power is not his own but Christ's. The verse marks the first raising of the dead by an apostle, and the manner is instructive: solitude, prayer, dependence on God, and then the word of power. Dorcas opens her eyes and sits up — restored to life and to her ministry of mercy. The servant prays; the Lord raises; the name of Christ is glorified in the gift of life.
41:
"And he gave her his hand, and lifted her up, and when he had called the saints and widows, presented her alive." The witnesses note the completion of the miracle and its presentation. The grammatical voices note the parallel with Christ. Chrysostom (under v.26): "So did Christ to the daughter of Jairus: having taken her by the hand. Mark severally, first the life, then the strength brought into her, the one by the word, the other by his hand." Peter's hand lifted up the one to whom life had been given by the word.

On the presentation, Barnes notes the parallel with Elijah (1 Kings 17:23): "He exhibited, or showed her to them alive." The witnesses note the joy of the saints and widows who received back their benefactress alive.

The Reformed Calvin notes the purpose of the raising — for others' sake and the glory of Christ: "she was raised again, rather for other men's sake than for her own." He answers those who object that it was unkind to call her back from blessed rest: "it is not lawful for God to have respect of his glory as well in death as in life," and her restoration made her "a more excellent instrument of God's goodness and power."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter gave Dorcas his hand, lifted her up, and presented her alive to the saints and widows (as Christ took Jairus's daughter by the hand, and as Elijah presented the widow's son, Chrysostom, Barnes). The joy of those who received back their benefactress alive crowned the miracle. Calvin notes the purpose: she was raised for others' sake and the glory of Christ — and though called back from blessed rest, her restoration made her a more excellent instrument of God's goodness. The verse displays the completion and the fruit of the miracle: Dorcas restored, lifted up by the apostle's hand, presented living to the weeping widows now turned to joy. The raising was not for Dorcas's sake alone (who passed from rest to renewed labor) but for the church's good and the glory of Christ. The verse marks the tender close of the scene: the dead disciple given back alive to those who mourned her, her ministry of mercy restored. The God who pities the poor (v.39) gives back their benefactress; and the name of Christ is glorified in the gift of life. The widows who showed the garments in tears now receive their maker alive — and the miracle turns many to faith (v.42).
42:
"And it was known throughout all Joppa; and many believed in the Lord." The witnesses note the wide effect of the miracle. Barnes: "This was the first miracle of this kind that was performed by the apostles. The effect was, that many believed." He compares the raising of Lazarus, which also led many to believe (John 12:11).

The grammatical voices note the spread of the report through all Joppa (a large city, Gill). The raising of a well-known and beloved disciple could not be hidden, and the report led many to faith in the Lord.

The Reformed Calvin sums up the manifold fruit of the miracle: "Now appear manifold fruits of the miracle, for God comforted the poor, a godly matron was restored to the Church, in whose death it suffered great loss, and many are called unto the faith." And he notes Peter's faithfulness: "although Peter were a minister of so great power, yet he keepeth not the men in himself; but doth rather direct them unto Christ." The faith of the many terminated not on Peter but on the Lord.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the raising of Dorcas was known throughout all Joppa, and many believed in the Lord — the first apostolic raising of the dead, leading (like the raising of Lazarus, John 12:11) to many conversions (Barnes). Calvin sums up the manifold fruit: the poor comforted, a godly matron restored, the church's loss repaired, and many called to faith; and he notes that the faith of the many terminated not on Peter but on the Lord, to whom Peter directed them. The verse displays the crowning purpose of the miracle: not merely the restoration of one disciple but the turning of many to faith in Christ. The sign confirmed the word, and the report of so great a wonder, wrought on a beloved disciple in a large city, could not be hidden. The verse marks the gospel's advance through Peter's ministry at Joppa, as at Lydda (v.35): the miracle that glorified Christ now gathers a harvest of souls. Peter, the faithful minister, claims nothing for himself but directs the believers to the Lord. The raising of Dorcas — comforting the poor, restoring the church, and converting many — displays the manifold goodness of God, who turns even death into an occasion of faith. Many believed in the Lord, and the church at Joppa grew.
43:
"And it came to pass, that he tarried many days in Joppa with one Simon a tanner." The brief verse notes Peter's lodging with a tanner. The grammatical voices note the significance of the tanner's trade. The witnesses observe that tanning was regarded by the Jews as an unclean and despised occupation (involving contact with dead animals), so that Peter's lodging there marks both his humility and a providential preparation.

Chrysostom (under v.26) notes the humility: "Mark the unassuming conduct, mark the moderation of Peter, how he does not make his abode with this lady, or some other person of distinction, but with a tanner: by all his acts leading men to humility, neither suffering the mean to be ashamed, nor the great to be elated."

The witnesses note the providential placing. Peter's stay at Joppa with Simon the tanner sets the stage for the great vision and the conversion of Cornelius (ch. 10): it was here that the messengers from Caesarea would find him, and here that the vision of the sheet would teach him that nothing God has cleansed is common. The tanner's house, where a Jew of strict scruple might hesitate to lodge, becomes the place where the barriers between Jew and Gentile begin to fall.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Peter tarried many days at Joppa with Simon a tanner — a trade the Jews despised as unclean (contact with dead animals), so that his lodging there marks both his humility (not seeking distinguished hosts but the lowly, Chrysostom) and a providential preparation. For it was here, in the tanner's house, that the messengers from Cornelius would find Peter (ch. 10), and here that the vision teaching him to call no man common would come. The verse displays Peter's unassuming character and the quiet working of providence: the apostle, lodging with a despised tanner, is being prepared, all unknowing, for the great step of carrying the gospel to the Gentiles. The verse closes the account of Peter's ministry at Lydda and Joppa and sets the stage for the conversion of Cornelius — the opening of the door of faith to the Gentiles. The tanner's house, where Jewish scruple might balk, becomes the threshold of the gospel's advance beyond Israel. Peter's humility in lodging there is itself a foreshadowing of the lesson he must learn: that in Christ the old distinctions of clean and unclean are passing away. The chapter ends with the apostle providentially placed for the next great work of God.
